title: All ears \br Todo ouvidos
example1: Tell me your idea – I'm all ears. \br She was all ears when the teacher explained the surprise.

title: As easy as ABC \br Fácil como comer pastel
example1: This recipe is as easy as ABC. \br The instructions were as easy as ABC – even a child could follow them.

title: Be broke \br Estar duro / sem grana
example1: I can't go out tonight, I'm broke. \br After buying the car, he was completely broke.

title: Be up to someone \br Depende de alguém
example1: It's up to you whether we go or stay. \br It's up to the manager to approve the budget.

title: Blow your nose \br Assoar o nariz
definition: To clear your nose by forcing air through it.
example1: Please blow your nose instead of sniffing. \br He blew his nose loudly during the concert.

title: Catch a cold \br Pegar um resfriado
definition: To become ill with a cold.
example1: Put on a jacket or you'll catch a cold. \br She caught a cold after walking in the rain.

title: Change your mind \br Mudar de ideia
example1: I was going to study law, but I changed my mind. \br He changed his mind at the last minute and stayed home.

title: Do someone a favour \br Fazer um favor a alguém
example1: Could you do me a favour and turn off the lights? \br She did me a huge favour by covering my shift.

title: Eat out \br Comer fora (restaurante)
example1: Let's eat out tonight – I don't feel like cooking. \br They eat out every Friday night.

title: Fall in love \br Apaixonar-se
example1: They fell in love while travelling in Italy. \br He fell in love with her the moment they met.

title: Get along with \br Dar-se bem com
example1: I get along well with my colleagues. \br Do you get along with your new roommate?

title: Go on foot \br Ir a pé
definition: To walk instead of using transport.
example1: The shop is near; let's go on foot. \br We went on foot to save money on taxi.

title: Have a good time \br Divertir-se
example1: We had a good time at the party. \br Did you have a good time at the beach?

title: Keep in touch \br Manter contacto
example1: Promise you'll keep in touch after you move. \br Even though they live far apart, they keep in touch regularly.

title: Make a decision \br Tomar uma decisão
example1: You need to make a decision by Friday. \br She finally made a decision about which university to attend.

title: Make a mistake \br Cometer um erro
example1: I made a mistake on the address. \br Everyone makes mistakes sometimes.

title: Make sure \br Certificar-se
example1: Make sure you lock the door. \br I made sure to turn off the oven before leaving.

title: On time \br Na hora certa
example1: The train arrived on time. \br Please submit your report on time.

title: Take a break \br Fazer uma pausa
example1: Let's take a break for ten minutes. \br You've been studying for hours – take a break.

title: Take care of \br Cuidar de
example1: She takes care of her elderly mother. \br Who will take care of your cat while you're away?

title: Take part in \br Participar de
example1: I took part in the school play. \br Many countries took part in the international competition.

title: Take turns \br Revezar-se
example1: Take turns using the computer. \br The children took turns on the swing.

title: Wake up \br Acordar
example1: I usually wake up at 7 a.m. \br He woke up late and missed the bus.

title: Work out \br Exercitar-se
example1: He works out at the gym three times a week. \br I need to work out more often to stay fit.

title: Worry about \br Preocupar-se com
example1: Don't worry about the exam – you'll do fine. \br She worries about her children all the time.

title: Write down \br Anotar
example1: Write down your phone number for me. \br I wrote down all the important dates in my calendar.

title: A piece of cake \br Muito fácil
example1: The test was a piece of cake. \br After years of practice, that task was a piece of cake.

title: As far as I know \br Até onde sei
example1: As far as I know, she still works here. \br As far as I know, the meeting is still on for tomorrow.

title: Be about to \br Estar prestes a
definition: To be ready to do something soon.
example1: We were about to leave when the phone rang. \br She's about to give birth any day now.

title: Be into something \br Estar a fim de / curtir
example1: She's really into photography. \br He's into vintage cars and motorcycles.

title: Be over \br Ter acabado
example1: The storm is finally over. \br The game was over by 9 PM.

title: Be supposed to \br Supostamente / dever
definition: To be expected or required to.
example1: You're supposed to arrive by 9 a.m. \br I'm supposed to call my boss later.

title: Beat around the bush \br Enrolar / evitar o assunto
example1: Stop beating around the bush – just tell me. \br He beat around the bush for an hour without giving an answer.

title: Better late than never \br Antes tarde do que nunca
definition: Doing something late is better than not at all.
example1: He apologized after years – better late than never. \br I finished the project a week late, but better late than never.

title: Break a leg \br Boa sorte! (teatro)
example1: You're on in five minutes – break a leg! \br Before the audition, her friend whispered 'break a leg'.

title: Break up \br Terminar (relacionamento)
example1: They broke up after two years together. \br She broke up with her boyfriend last week.

title: Call it a day \br Dar o dia por encerrado
definition: To stop working for the day.
example1: It's 6 p.m., let's call it a day. \br After finishing the report, we called it a day.

title: Come on \br Vamos lá / qual é
example1: Come on, you can do it! \br Come on, you don't really believe that, do you?

title: Cost an arm and a leg \br Custar os olhos da cara
example1: The repair cost an arm and a leg. \br Their new sofa cost an arm and a leg, but it's beautiful.

title: Cut it out \br Para com isso
example1: Cut it out, you're bothering me! \br I wish the kids would cut it out and be quiet.

title: Do your best \br Fazer o melhor possível
definition: To try as hard as you can.
example1: Just do your best on the exam. \br She always does her best in every project.

title: Feel like \br Estar com vontade de
example1: I feel like having pizza tonight. \br Do you feel like going for a walk?

title: Figure out \br Descobrir / entender
example1: I can't figure out this math problem. \br Have you figured out how to use the new software?

title: Get lost \br Perder-se / sumir (gír.)
definition: To become lost (or rude: go away).
example1: We got lost on the way to the museum. \br Get lost! I don't want to talk to you.

title: Get married \br Casar-se
example1: They got married in a small church. \br When are you going to get married?

title: Get rid of \br Livrar-se de
example1: I need to get rid of these old clothes. \br He got rid of his old phone and bought a new one.

title: Give up \br Desistir
example1: Don't give up – you're almost there. \br She gave up smoking last year.

title: Go ahead \br Vá em frente / prossiga
example1: Can I open the window? – Go ahead. \br The manager told us to go ahead with the plan.

title: Hang out \br Sair / passar tempo com
definition: To spend time casually with friends.
example1: I hang out with my friends on Saturdays. \br We used to hang out at the mall after school.

title: Have a look \br Dar uma olhada
example1: Have a look at this photo. \br Could you have a look at my computer? It's not working.

title: Have no idea \br Não fazer ideia
example1: I have no idea where my keys are. \br He has no idea how to fix the car.

title: Hold on \br Aguente / espere
example1: Hold on a second, I'll be right back. \br Hold on to the railing so you don't fall.

title: In a hurry \br Com pressa
example1: Sorry, I can't talk – I'm in a hurry. \br She finished her lunch in a hurry and left.

title: In the end \br No fim das contas
example1: In the end, we decided to stay home. \br In the end, everything worked out fine.

title: It doesn't matter \br Não importa
example1: You're late? It doesn't matter. \br It doesn't matter which colour you choose – they're all nice.

title: Keep an eye on \br Ficar de olho em
example1: Could you keep an eye on my bag? \br Keep an eye on the stove while I answer the door.

title: Look forward to \br Estar ansioso para
definition: To be excited about a future event.
example1: I look forward to seeing you. \br We're looking forward to our vacation next month.

title: Look up \br Pesquisar
example1: Look up the word in a dictionary. \br I looked up the recipe online.

title: Make fun of \br Tirar sarro de
example1: Don't make fun of his accent. \br They made fun of her new haircut.

title: Make up your mind \br Decidir-se
example1: Make up your mind – which one do you want? \br He can't make up his mind about which job to take.

title: No matter what \br Não importa o que aconteça
example1: I'll support you no matter what. \br No matter what happens, don't lose hope.

title: On purpose \br De propósito
example1: He stepped on my foot on purpose. \br I didn't break the vase on purpose – it was an accident.

title: Out of order \br Fora de serviço
example1: The elevator is out of order. \br The vending machine is out of order today.

title: Put off \br Adiar
example1: We put off the meeting until Friday. \br Don't put off your homework until the last minute.

title: Run out of \br Ficar sem
definition: To have no more of something.
example1: We ran out of milk. \br We ran out of time before finishing the test.

title: Show up \br Aparecer
example1: He showed up late again. \br I waited for an hour but she never showed up.

title: So far \br Até agora
example1: So far, everything is going well. \br So far, we've raised $500 for charity.

title: Take advantage of \br Aproveitar-se de
example1: He took advantage of the sale to buy cheap shoes. \br She took advantage of his kindness and asked for more money.

title: Take it easy \br Levar na boa / relaxar
example1: Take it easy – you're stressed. \br After the exam, I plan to take it easy for a few days.

title: Take your time \br Sem pressa
example1: You can take your time finishing the report. \br Take your time – there's no deadline today.

title: Turn down \br Recusar
example1: She turned down the job offer. \br He asked her to dance, but she turned him down.

title: Under the weather \br Sentindo-se mal (doente)
example1: I'm feeling a bit under the weather today. \br He stayed home because he was under the weather.

title: Ups and downs \br Altos e baixos
example1: Life has its ups and downs. \br Our relationship has had many ups and downs over the years.

title: Use up \br Esgotar / gastar todo
example1: We used up all the toothpaste. \br She used up her savings to buy a car.

title: What's up? \br E aí? / O que houve?
example1: Hey, what's up? \br What's up? You look worried.

title: Work on \br Trabalhar em / melhorar
definition: To try to improve or fix.
example1: I need to work on my pronunciation. \br He's working on a new project at the office.

title: Would rather \br Preferir
example1: I'd rather stay home than go out. \br She would rather read a book than watch TV.

title: You bet! \br Com certeza!
example1: Do you want dessert? – You bet! \br Are you coming to the party? You bet I am!

title: You wish! \br Quem dera! / Sonha!
example1: You think you'll win? You wish! \br He hopes to get a promotion – you wish!

title: A blessing in disguise \br Um mal que vem para o bem
definition: Something bad that leads to good.
example1: Losing that job was a blessing in disguise. \br The delay was a blessing in disguise because it made us prepare better.

title: A dime a dozen \br Tem aos montes
example1: Tourists are a dime a dozen here. \br Those cheap souvenirs are a dime a dozen.

title: Add insult to injury \br Por sal na ferida
definition: To make a bad situation worse.
example1: He insulted me, then to add insult to injury, he laughed. \br First they cancelled our flight, and then to add insult to injury, they refused a refund.

title: At the drop of a hat \br Num piscar de olhos
example1: He'd help me at the drop of a hat. \br She'd pack her bags and travel at the drop of a hat.

title: Back to square one \br Voltar à estaca zero
example1: The plan failed, so it's back to square one. \br After the crash, we lost all data and had to go back to square one.

title: Be all talk \br Só falar, não fazer
example1: He says he'll help, but he's all talk. \br Don't believe her promises – she's all talk.

title: Be in hot water \br Estar enrascado
example1: He's in hot water for missing the deadline. \br I'll be in hot water if my boss finds out.

title: Be on the fence \br Estar em cima do muro
example1: I'm on the fence about moving abroad. \br Many voters are still on the fence about the candidate.

title: Be over the moon \br Estar nas nuvens
example1: She was over the moon about her promotion. \br He was over the moon when he got the job.

title: Be up in the air \br Estar no ar
example1: Our vacation plans are still up in the air. \br The future of the project is up in the air.

title: Bite the bullet \br Encarar o sofrimento
definition: To do something unpleasant but necessary.
example1: I hate public speaking, but I'll bite the bullet. \br He bit the bullet and apologized to his boss.

title: Break the ice \br Quebrar o gelo
definition: To start a conversation in a stiff situation.
example1: A joke is good to break the ice. \br He told a funny story to break the ice at the meeting.

title: Burn the midnight oil \br Queimar o óleo da meia-noite
example1: She burned the midnight oil for the exam. \br We burned the midnight oil to finish the proposal on time.

title: By the skin of your teeth \br Por um triz
example1: I passed by the skin of my teeth. \br He escaped the accident by the skin of his teeth.

title: Call it a night \br Dar a noite por encerrada
example1: We've played enough – let's call it a night. \br After the movie, we decided to call it a night.

title: Catch someone's eye \br Chamar a atenção de alguém
example1: That painting caught my eye. \br A bright red car caught her eye from across the street.

title: Cut corners \br Cortar caminhos (fazer nas coxas)
definition: To do something poorly to save time or money.
example1: The builder cut corners and the roof leaks. \br Don't cut corners on safety procedures.

title: Cut someone some slack \br Dar um desconto
example1: He's new – cut him some slack. \br Everyone makes mistakes – cut her some slack.

title: Drive someone up the wall \br Deixar alguém louco
example1: His loud chewing drives me up the wall. \br That constant beeping noise is driving me up the wall.

title: Every cloud has a silver lining \br Não há mal que sempre dure
definition: There is something good in every bad situation.
example1: Don't worry – every cloud has a silver lining. \br He lost his job, but then found a better one – every cloud has a silver lining.

title: Face the music \br Encarar as consequências
example1: You cheated – now face the music. \br After breaking the window, he had to face the music.

title: Get a kick out of \br Divertir-se com
example1: I get a kick out of watching old movies. \br She gets a kick out of playing practical jokes.

title: Get out of hand \br Sair do controle
example1: The party got out of hand. \br The argument got out of hand and the police were called.

title: Get something off your chest \br Tirar um peso do peito
example1: I need to get this off my chest: I was wrong. \br He finally got his frustrations off his chest and felt better.

title: Get your act together \br Se organizar
example1: You need to get your act together and study. \br The team got their act together and won the championship.

title: Give the benefit of the doubt \br Dar o benefício da dúvida
example1: I don't know, but I'll give her the benefit of the doubt. \br Even though he was late, his boss gave him the benefit of the doubt.

title: Go back to the drawing board \br Voltar à prancheta
example1: The client rejected it – back to the drawing board. \br Our plan didn't work, so we had to go back to the drawing board.

title: Go the extra mile \br Dar o sangue / ir além
example1: He goes the extra mile for his customers. \br If you want to succeed, be willing to go the extra mile.

title: Hang in there \br Aguenta firme
example1: The first month is hard – hang in there. \br I know you're struggling, but hang in there – things will get better.

title: Have a sweet tooth \br Ser formigueiro
example1: She has a sweet tooth and loves chocolate. \br I have a sweet tooth, so I always order dessert.

title: Have your hands full \br Estar com as mãos ocupadas
example1: I have my hands full with three kids. \br She can't take on another project – her hands are full.

title: Hit the books \br Cair nos livros
example1: I need to hit the books for finals. \br If you don't hit the books, you won't pass the test.

title: Hit the hay / sack \br Cair na cama
example1: I'm exhausted – time to hit the hay. \br It's late – let's hit the sack.

title: Hit the nail on the head \br Acertar em cheio
example1: You hit the nail on the head about the problem. \br His analysis hit the nail on the head.

title: In the same boat \br No mesmo barco
example1: We're all in the same boat with the new software. \br If we lose our jobs, we'll all be in the same boat.

title: It takes two to tango \br É preciso dois para dançar tango
example1: Don't blame only him – it takes two to tango. \br They both contributed to the argument – it takes two to tango.

title: Let the cat out of the bag \br Soltar o verbo / contar o segredo
example1: I accidentally let the cat out of the bag about the party. \br He let the cat out of the bag about the surprise promotion.

title: Make a long story short \br Resumindo a história
definition: To tell only the main points.
example1: To make a long story short, we missed the flight. \br He made a long story short and gave us the highlights.

title: Miss the boat \br Perder a oportunidade
example1: I waited too long and missed the boat. \br If you don't apply now, you'll miss the boat.

title: No pain no gain \br Sem dor sem ganho
definition: You must work hard to succeed.
example1: I trained for months – no pain, no gain. \br If you want to get fit, remember: no pain, no gain.

title: On the ball \br Esperto / atento
example1: My assistant is really on the ball. \br You have to be on the ball to succeed in sales.

title: Once in a blue moon \br De vez em nunca
example1: He visits once in a blue moon. \br I go to the cinema once in a blue moon now that I have kids.

title: Pull someone's leg \br Tirar o sarro / brincar
example1: I'm just pulling your leg – it's not true. \br Are you serious or are you pulling my leg?

title: Ring a bell \br Lembrar vagamente
example1: His name rings a bell, but I'm not sure. \br That song rings a bell from my childhood.

title: See eye to eye \br Estar de acordo
example1: We don't see eye to eye on politics. \br I'm glad we finally see eye to eye on this issue.

title: Speak of the devil \br Falar no diabo (e aparece)
definition: When someone appears after being discussed.
example1: Speak of the devil! We were just talking about you. \br I was thinking about calling you, and speak of the devil – you phoned!

title: Steal someone's thunder \br Roubar a atenção
definition: To take credit or attention from someone.
example1: She announced her pregnancy at my wedding – she stole my thunder. \br He stole my thunder by sharing my idea before I could.

title: Straight from the horse's mouth \br Da boca do cavalo
example1: I heard it straight from the horse's mouth. \br The news came straight from the horse's mouth – the CEO himself.

title: The ball is in your court \br A bola está com você
example1: I made an offer – now the ball is in your court. \br I've done my part, so the ball is in your court now.

title: The best of both worlds \br O melhor dos dois mundos
example1: Working from home gives me the best of both worlds. \br This hybrid car offers the best of both worlds: electric and petrol.

title: The last straw \br A gota d'água
example1: His lateness was the last straw – I fired him. \br The third complaint was the last straw; they closed the restaurant.

title: Through thick and thin \br Nas horas boas e ruins
example1: We've been friends through thick and thin. \br They supported each other through thick and thin.

title: Throw in the towel \br Jogar a toalha
example1: After three failures, he threw in the towel. \br Don't throw in the towel just yet – you still have a chance.

title: Up to scratch \br À altura (do padrão)
example1: His work isn't up to scratch. \br We need to improve our customer service to bring it up to scratch.

title: When pigs fly \br Quando as galinhas tiverem dentes
example1: He'll clean his room when pigs fly. \br She'll agree to that when pigs fly.

title: You can say that again \br Diga isso de novo (concordo muito)
example1: This heat is unbearable. – You can say that again! \br This is the best cake I've ever had. – You can say that again!

title: A bird's eye view \br Vista aérea
example1: The drone gave us a bird's eye view. \br From the rooftop, we had a bird's eye view of the whole city.

title: A sight for sore eyes \br Um alívio para os olhos
definition: Someone or something you're glad to see.
example1: After the hike, the hotel was a sight for sore eyes. \br When she walked in, she was a sight for sore eyes.

title: As the crow flies \br Em linha reta
example1: It's 5 miles as the crow flies, but 7 by road. \br The two towns are close as the crow flies, but separated by a river.

title: Back to the wall \br Encurralado
definition: In a difficult situation with few options.
example1: With his back to the wall, he signed the contract. \br The company had its back to the wall and had to accept the merger.

title: Beat a dead horse \br Bater em cavalo morto
definition: To waste effort on a settled issue.
example1: We already decided – you're beating a dead horse. \br Stop arguing about it; you're beating a dead horse.

title: Bite the hand that feeds you \br Morder a mão que te alimenta
definition: To harm someone who helps you.
example1: He sued his own father – biting the hand that feeds you. \br Don't complain about your boss publicly; that would be biting the hand that feeds you.

title: Burn the candle at both ends \br Gastar a vela dos dois lados
definition: To overwork by sleeping little and working much.
example1: He's burning the candle at both ends and looks tired. \br She's been burning the candle at both ends to finish her thesis.

title: Bury the hatchet \br Enterrar o machado
example1: After years of fighting, they finally buried the hatchet. \br Let's bury the hatchet and be friends again.

title: By the book \br Pela regra
example1: Our accountant does everything by the book. \br The police officer did everything by the book during the arrest.

title: Castles in the air \br Castelos no ar
example1: His plan to be a millionaire is just castles in the air. \br Stop building castles in the air and focus on achievable goals.

title: Come full circle \br Fechar o círculo
definition: To return to an original position.
example1: His career came full circle when he rejoined the startup. \br After many adventures, the story came full circle.

title: Cry over spilt milk \br Chorar sobre leite derramado
definition: To be upset about something unchangeable.
example1: No use crying over spilt milk – let's move on. \br He failed the test, but crying over spilt milk won't help.

title: Cut to the quick \br Cortar no fundo da alma
example1: Her words cut him to the quick. \br The criticism cut her to the quick, and she left the room in tears.

title: Dark horse \br Cavalo azarão (surpresa)
example1: He was a dark horse and won the election. \br The dark horse of the tournament beat all the favourites.

title: Do justice to \br Fazer justiça a
example1: This photo doesn't do justice to the view. \br The movie did justice to the original book.

title: Don't put all your eggs in one basket \br Não colocar todos os ovos na mesma cesta
definition: Don't risk everything on one thing.
example1: Invest in different assets – don't put all eggs in one basket. \br Apply to several universities, not just one. Don't put all your eggs in one basket.

title: Drive a hard bargain \br Negociar duro
example1: She drove a hard bargain and got a discount. \br He's known for driving a hard bargain, but he's fair.

title: Drop in the bucket \br Gota no oceano
example1: Our donation is a drop in the bucket. \br Ten dollars is a drop in the bucket compared to what we need.

title: Eleventh hour \br Última hora
example1: They reached an agreement at the eleventh hour. \br The rescue team arrived at the eleventh hour.

title: Every Tom Dick and Harry \br Todo Zé, Mané e Joaquim
example1: This hotel is for everyone, not every Tom, Dick and Harry. \br You can't let every Tom, Dick and Harry into your private office.

title: Get your ducks in a row \br Colocar as coisas em ordem
example1: Before you launch, get your ducks in a row. \br We need to get our ducks in a row before presenting the proposal.

title: Give the cold shoulder \br Dar gelo
example1: She gave me the cold shoulder after the argument. \br When I said hello, he gave me the cold shoulder.

title: Go for broke \br Arriscar tudo
definition: To risk everything in an all-out effort.
example1: It's the final round – go for broke! \br He went for broke and invested his life savings in the business.

title: Have a lot on your plate \br Ter muito prato (estar sobrecarregado)
example1: I can't help – I have a lot on my plate. \br She has a lot on her plate with work and school.

title: Hear it on the grapevine \br Ouvir no boca a boca
example1: I heard on the grapevine that you're leaving. \br He heard on the grapevine that the company was being sold.

title: Hold your horses \br Segurar os cavalos
example1: Hold your horses – we can't leave yet. \br The teacher told the excited students to hold their horses.

title: If the shoe fits wear it \br Se a carapuça serviu veste
definition: If a criticism applies to you, accept it.
example1: I'm not saying you're lazy, but if the shoe fits... \br He was offended by the comment about dishonesty – well, if the shoe fits...

title: Jump on the bandwagon \br Ir na onda
example1: Many brands jumped on the sustainability bandwagon. \br After the movie became a hit, everyone jumped on the bandwagon.

title: Keep your chin up \br Manter a cabeça erguida
example1: Keep your chin up – things will improve. \br Even after losing the match, he kept his chin up.

title: Kill two birds with one stone \br Matar dois coelhos com uma só cajadada
definition: To achieve two things with one action.
example1: I'll shop on the way home – kill two birds with one stone. \br By studying while commuting, she kills two birds with one stone.

title: Leave no stone unturned \br Virar todas as pedras
example1: The police left no stone unturned. \br We'll leave no stone unturned to find the missing dog.

title: Let sleeping dogs lie \br Não mexer em vespeiro
example1: Don't ask about their divorce – let sleeping dogs lie. \br I wanted to confront him, but my friend said to let sleeping dogs lie.

title: Make ends meet \br Fazer as contas fecharem
example1: With two jobs, we barely make ends meet. \br Many families struggle to make ends meet.

title: Make hay while the sun shines \br Fazer feno enquanto o sol brilha
definition: To take advantage of good conditions.
example1: Sales are high – make hay while the sun shines. \br The weather is perfect, so let's make hay while the sun shines and finish the outdoor work.

title: Off the record \br Em off (não oficial)
example1: He told me off the record that the CEO is leaving. \br The minister spoke off the record to the journalist.

title: On cloud nine \br Nas nuvens
example1: She's been on cloud nine since her wedding. \br When he got the promotion, he was on cloud nine.

title: On thin ice \br Pisar em ovos
example1: You're on thin ice with your boss. \br After his third warning, he knew he was on thin ice.

title: Once bitten twice shy \br Gato escaldado tem medo de água fria
definition: After a bad experience, you're cautious.
example1: He won't lend money again – once bitten, twice shy. \br She never invests in risky stocks after losing money – once bitten, twice shy.

title: Out of the blue \br Do nada
example1: She called me out of the blue after ten years. \br The job offer came out of the blue.

title: Play devil's advocate \br Advogado do diabo
definition: To argue the opposite side for debate.
example1: I agree, but let me play devil's advocate. \br He often plays devil's advocate to challenge our assumptions.

title: Pull yourself together \br Se recompor
example1: Stop crying – pull yourself together. \br After the shock, he needed a moment to pull himself together.

title: Rain on someone's parade \br Estragar a festa
example1: Sorry to rain on your parade, but we need to work. \br I don't want to rain on your parade, but that plan has some problems.

title: Sit on the fence \br Ficar em cima do muro
example1: You can't sit on the fence – choose a side. \br Politicians often sit on the fence to avoid controversy.

title: Skeleton in the closet \br Esqueleto no armário
example1: Every politician has a skeleton in the closet. \br He was afraid someone would discover the skeleton in his closet.

title: Spill the beans \br Derramar o feijão (contar segredo)
example1: Who spilled the beans about the surprise party? \br I trusted her not to spill the beans, but she told everyone.

title: The tip of the iceberg \br A ponta do iceberg
definition: Only a small part of a larger problem.
example1: The scandals are just the tip of the iceberg. \br The budget cuts we saw are only the tip of the iceberg.

title: Throw caution to the wind \br Jogar cautela ao vento
example1: He threw caution to the wind and quit his job. \br Sometimes you have to throw caution to the wind and take a risk.

title: Up in the air \br No ar
example1: Our travel plans are still up in the air. \br The future of the project remains up in the air.

title: Walk on eggshells \br Pisar em ovos
definition: To act carefully to avoid upsetting someone.
example1: I walk on eggshells around my boss. \br Since their argument, they've been walking on eggshells around each other.

title: When it rains it pours \br Desgraça pouca é bobagem
example1: First the car broke, then I got sick – when it rains, it pours. \br He lost his job, his wife left him, and then his house flooded. When it rains, it pours.

title: You can't have your cake and eat it too \br Não dá para ter o bolo e comê-lo
definition: You cannot have two incompatible things.
example1: You want low taxes and great services – you can't have your cake and eat it too. \br He wants to travel the world and save money at the same time – you can't have your cake and eat it too.

title: A drop in the ocean \br Gota no oceano
definition: Very small compared to what is needed.
example1: Our effort is a drop in the ocean. \br A hundred dollars is a drop in the ocean for that project.

title: A fool's errand \br Missão impossível
example1: Trying to convince him is a fool's errand. \br Fixing that old car would be a fool's errand – it's beyond repair.

title: A leopard can't change its spots \br Pau que nasce torto nunca se endireita
example1: He's still dishonest – a leopard can't change its spots. \br You think he'll stop lying? A leopard can't change its spots.

title: A wolf in sheep's clothing \br Lobo em pele de cordeiro
definition: A dangerous person pretending to be harmless.
example1: Beware of him – he's a wolf in sheep's clothing. \br The charming stranger turned out to be a wolf in sheep's clothing.

title: Achilles' heel \br Calcanhar de Aquiles
example1: His poor eyesight is his Achilles' heel. \br For all his talent, his temper is his Achilles' heel.

title: Barking up the wrong tree \br Latir para a árvore errada
definition: To be mistaken about the source.
example1: If you think I stole it, you're barking up the wrong tree. \br The police were barking up the wrong tree with their main suspect.

title: Beat around the bush \br Enrolar
example1: Stop beating around the bush – tell me directly. \br He always beats around the bush when asked about his plans.

title: Bed of roses \br Mar de rosas
example1: Life isn't a bed of roses. \br Running a business is no bed of roses, but it's rewarding.

title: Beyond the pale \br Fora dos limites
example1: His behavior was beyond the pale. \br That kind of insult is beyond the pale in a professional setting.

title: Bite your tongue \br Morder a língua
example1: I had to bite my tongue to not yell. \br When he criticized my work, I bit my tongue and stayed quiet.

title: Blow off steam \br Desabafar
example1: He goes for a run to blow off steam. \br After a long day, she calls a friend to blow off steam.

title: By the same token \br Pela mesma razão
example1: He is lazy; by the same token, he's unreliable. \br She's very talented; by the same token, she's also very demanding.

title: Call a spade a spade \br Dar nome aos bois
example1: I like her because she calls a spade a spade. \br Let's call a spade a spade – the project failed because of poor leadership.

title: Can't see the forest for the trees \br Ver a árvore e não a floresta
example1: You're too focused on details – you can't see the forest for the trees. \br His obsession with minor mistakes meant he couldn't see the forest for the trees.

title: Carry weight \br Ter peso / influência
example1: His opinion carries weight. \br When she speaks, her words carry weight because of her experience.

title: Champ at the bit \br Estar ansioso para começar
example1: The team was champing at the bit to begin. \br The horses were champing at the bit before the race.

title: Close but no cigar \br Quase, mas não foi dessa vez
example1: Your guess was close but no cigar. \br He almost won the competition – close but no cigar.

title: Cross that bridge when you come to it \br Atravessar a ponte quando chegar nela
definition: To deal with problems when they occur.
example1: Worry later – we'll cross that bridge when we come to it. \br I don't know what will happen if we fail, but I'll cross that bridge when I come to it.

title: Cut the mustard \br Dar conta do recado
example1: He doesn't cut the mustard as a manager. \br If you can't cut the mustard, you'll be replaced.

title: Die hard \br Morrer difícil (teimoso)
example1: Old habits die hard. \br His prejudices die hard; he still won't accept the new policy.

title: Don't count your chickens before they hatch \br Não contar com o ovo no cu da galinha
example1: You haven't got the job yet – don't count your chickens. \br She's already planning how to spend her bonus, but she should not count her chickens before they hatch.

title: Dutch courage \br Coragem da cachaça
example1: He had a beer for Dutch courage. \br Some people need Dutch courage before speaking in public.

title: Eleventh hour \br Última hora
example1: The rescue came at the eleventh hour. \br They signed the deal at the eleventh hour.

title: Flesh and blood \br Carne e sangue
example1: I have to help my own flesh and blood. \br It's only flesh and blood to feel angry in that situation.

title: For all the tea in China \br Nem por todo o ouro do mundo
example1: I wouldn't do it for all the tea in China. \br He wouldn't apologize for all the tea in China.

title: Get the short end of the stick \br Levar a pior
example1: I always get the short end of the stick. \br In the divorce, he got the short end of the stick.

title: Give the shirt off your back \br Dar a camisa
example1: He'd give the shirt off his back to a stranger. \br She's so kind – she would give the shirt off her back to help anyone.

title: Go out on a limb \br Arriscar-se
example1: I'll go out on a limb and say he'll win. \br The manager went out on a limb to approve the risky project.

title: Hit the ground running \br Começar a todo vapor
definition: To start something with enthusiasm and skill.
example1: She hit the ground running on day one. \br The new employee hit the ground running and finished the task early.

title: In a nutshell \br Em poucas palavras
example1: In a nutshell, we lost. \br To put it in a nutshell, the project was a failure.

title: In the heat of the moment \br No calor do momento
example1: I said that in the heat of the moment. \br He didn't mean to insult you – it was in the heat of the moment.

title: Jump the gun \br Queimar a largada
example1: They jumped the gun and released the product before testing. \br Don't jump the gun – wait for the official announcement.

title: Let the chips fall where they may \br Deixe as coisas acontecerem
example1: I'll do my best and let the chips fall where they may. \br She spoke the truth and let the chips fall where they may.

title: Live and let live \br Viva e deixe viver
example1: We have different lifestyles – live and let live. \br He doesn't agree with their choices, but he believes in live and let live.

title: Make a mountain out of a molehill \br Transformar um grão de areia em montanha
example1: Calm down – you're making a mountain out of a molehill. \br She's making a mountain out of a molehill over a minor typo.

title: Not all that glitters is gold \br Nem tudo que reluz é ouro
example1: The job looks great, but not all that glitters is gold. \br The fancy car broke down after a week – not all that glitters is gold.

title: Play hard to get \br Fazer-se de difícil
example1: He's playing hard to get, but he likes her. \br If you play hard to get, they might become more interested.

title: Put your foot in your mouth \br Meter os pés pelas mãos
example1: I put my foot in my mouth by asking about her ex. \br He really put his foot in his mouth when he commented on her weight.

title: Rain check \br Deixar para outra hora
example1: Can I take a rain check on dinner? \br I'm busy tonight – can I take a rain check?

title: Read between the lines \br Ler nas entrelinhas
example1: His email was friendly, but reading between the lines, he's angry. \br If you read between the lines, she's actually asking for help.

title: Rome wasn't built in a day \br Roma não foi construída num dia
example1: Be patient – Rome wasn't built in a day. \br Don't expect to become fluent in a month; Rome wasn't built in a day.

title: Rub salt in the wound \br Esfregar sal na ferida
definition: To make a painful situation worse.
example1: He laughed about my failure – rubbing salt in the wound. \br After she lost her job, they asked about her salary – that just rubbed salt in the wound.

title: Sit on your hands \br Ficar de braços cruzados
definition: To do nothing when action is needed.
example1: The government sat on its hands during the crisis. \br We can't just sit on our hands while the problem gets worse.

title: The pot calling the kettle black \br O sujo falando do mal lavado
example1: He called me lazy – that's the pot calling the kettle black. \br She accused him of being messy – talk about the pot calling the kettle black.

title: Throw someone under the bus \br Jogar alguém na fogueira
example1: He blamed his colleague to save himself – threw him under the bus. \br I trusted him, but he threw me under the bus to get a promotion.

title: Turn a blind eye \br Fazer vista grossa
example1: The teacher turned a blind eye to the phone use. \br The guard turned a blind eye when the children snuck in.

title: Up the creek without a paddle \br Em apuros sem saída
example1: If the car breaks down, we'll be up the creek without a paddle. \br Without the loan, the company is up the creek without a paddle.

title: Wear your heart on your sleeve \br Usar o coração na manga
example1: She wears her heart on her sleeve – you always know how she feels. \br He doesn't hide his feelings; he wears his heart on his sleeve.

title: When in Rome do as the Romans do \br Em Roma seja como os romanos
example1: We eat dinner late here – when in Rome... \br When travelling, it's best to follow local customs – when in Rome, do as the Romans do.

title: You can't judge a book by its cover \br Não julgue o livro pela capa
example1: He looks rough, but you can't judge a book by its cover. \br The hotel looked shabby outside, but the rooms were lovely – you can't judge a book by its cover.
